alt:
Flash & ActionScript once made the web move.

Before HTML5 became the default answer to everything, Flash was everywhere.

Animations.
Browser games.
Interactive menus.
Cartoons.
E-learning.
Tiny loading bars that somehow felt like an event.

Flash started life in 1996 as FutureSplash Animator.
Later that year, Macromedia acquired it and renamed it Flash.

Then came the scripting.

First simple actions.
Then ActionScript.
Then ActionScript 2.0.
Then ActionScript 3.0, where Flash stopped being “j

Rubber duck debugging sounds ridiculous.

And that is exactly why it works.

The idea is simple:
you explain your code, line by line, to a rubber duck.

The duck says nothing.
The duck judges nothing.
The duck has no sprint planning after this.

But while explaining the problem out loud, something strange happens.

Your brain slows down.
Your assumptions become visible.
That vague “it should work” turns into actual logic.

And suddenly you hear it:

“Wait… why am I passing null there?”
“Why did

Curly braces feel so normal today that we barely notice them.

{
code lives here
}

But before C made them famous, **BCPL** was already putting code in braces.

BCPL stands for **Basic Combined Programming Language**.

A wonderfully practical name for a wonderfully practical language.

It was designed by Martin Richards in the 1960s as a simpler descendant of CPL, focused on portability, compiler writing and systems programming.

Not exactly a language built for hype.

More like:

“Can we make t

Before objects were cool, Simula already had classes.

Simula sounds almost too obvious.

And that is because it was.

The name comes from SIMUlation LAnguage: a language created to model and simulate systems, especially discrete-event systems.

In the early 1960s, Ole-Johan Dahl and Kristen Nygaard worked on it at the Norwegian Computing Center.

The goal was practical: describe systems, events, processes and behaviour in a way computers could actually run.

But then something bigger happened.

Xamarin sounds like a mysterious framework from a sci-fi lab.

Reality?

It was named after a monkey.

More precisely: the tamarin monkey.
The “T” became an “X”, keeping the playful naming tradition around Ximian, Mono, and the wider .NET ecosystem.

And honestly, it fits.

Xamarin’s mission was all about movement:
one codebase, multiple platforms, iOS and Android, C Sharp and .NET trying to keep everyone in the same tree.

Was it perfect?
No.

Was cross-platform mobile development ever peaceful

COBOL didn’t try to sound mysterious.
It basically showed up with a job description.

In 1959, the people behind COBOL needed a shared language for business data processing.
So they named it exactly that:

Common Business-Oriented Language.

Common: less tied to one machine.
Business-oriented: payroll, ledgers, reports, records.
Language: readable enough that humans could still recognize what was going on.

Not the coolest name in computing.
Possibly one of the most honest.

And more than 60 yea

Some programming languages become trendy.
Ada became trusted.

Built in the early 1980s, named after Ada Lovelace, and designed for systems where “oops, runtime surprise” is not exactly a feature.

Ada was created with reliability, strong typing, safety and maintainability in mind. That made it a natural fit for mission-critical domains like defense, avionics, aerospace and space-station software.

In 1995, Ada 95 became an international standard: ISO/IEC 8652:1995.

🍕🎮 Fun fact: Pac‑Man’s name started with a pizza! In 1980, Namco designer Toru Iwatani was hungry and doodled a pizza with one slice missing; that sketch became our yellow hero. He was first called ‘Puck‑Man’, after the Japanese onomatopoeia “paku paku” (the sound of munching), but for the U.S. release the name was tweaked to ‘Pac‑Man’ to deter mischievous arcade scribblers. Since then he’s been chomping dots and dodging ghosts as an arcade legend. Game over? Never.

🤖 Bombe-bastic Engineering! How #Turing Went Full 'Enigma-ineer' 💣💡

Back in WWII, Alan Turing and his team worked tirelessly to break the German #Enigma code. Their tool? The Bombe, an electro-mechanical machine that revolutionized cryptography and gave birth to modern computing. This incredible innovation was a precursor to the very systems we use today, shaping how we think about algorithms, data, and security.
